Party_Details.Name: Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd is a company incorporated in Singapore and holds all the issued shares in a New Zealand incorporated company called NQF Limited, formerly known as Newmans Quality Foods Limited.
Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd is a wholly-owned subsidiary of Newmans Group Holdings (Pty) Ltd, a company incorporated in Australia.
Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd was placed in creditors' voluntary liquidation and liquidators were appointed.
The common shareholders of Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd and NQF Limited include Wong Peng Koon and his son, Mark Wong Kuan Meng.
Wong Peng Koon and Mark Wong Kuan Meng are solicitors practising under the name PK Wong & Associates LLC from 2004-03-01.

On 13 February 2001, Henry George Townsing wrote to Wong Peng Koon to confirm that the Normandy Group and the CVC Group would subscribe for two million redeemable preference shares in Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd.
On 30 April 2001, the Redeemable Convertible Preference Share Agreement was executed between Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd and Normandy Nominees Limited.
The Redeemable Convertible Preference Share Agreement described Normandy Nominees Limited as a company incorporated in Hong Kong.
On 18 April 2001, Normandy Nominees Limited paid S$250,000 to Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd as part of a S$2,000,000 subscription for preference shares.
On 2 May 2001, Normandy Nominees Limited paid the balance S$1,750,000 to Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd, completing the S$2,000,000 subscription.
Out of the S$2,000,000 subscription sum, approximately S$1,125,000 was lent by Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd to its subsidiary, NQF Limited.
Under the preference share agreement, a limit of S$825,000 was set for Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd to repay bank facilities and advances made to it by its members.
Loans from various shareholders and the company's bank were repaid following the receipt of the subscription funds.
Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd appointed Henry George Townsing, nominated by Normandy Nominees Limited, as a director.
A Series 1 Agreement was executed providing that the S$2,000,000 debt owed to Normandy by Jenton was deemed repaid and treated as a loan from Normandy to the parent company, Newmans Group Holdings.
Newmans Group Holdings issued a Series 2 Loan Note and a subsequent rights issue to which Normandy Nominees Limited subscribed.
Normandy Nominees Limited performed a set-off of S$200,000 against a required deposit instead of making a full cash payment for its subscription.
Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd executed a deed of debenture in favour of its parent company, Newmans Group Holdings.
The debenture in favour of Newmans Group Holdings was not stamped or registered with the Registry of Companies in Singapore.
Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd executed a charge in favour of Normandy Nominees Limited, which was registered in Singapore.
A third-party creditor obtained a judgment against Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd for S$1,150,000.
Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd recorded increasing liabilities to various creditors, including debts related to interest on third-party loans.
A special resolution was passed to liquidate Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd, and a committee of inspection was appointed.
Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd asserted that it was owed S$4,452,284 by its subsidiary, NQF Limited.
Banks were notified of the appointment of liquidators over Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd.
Resolutions were adopted regarding the directorship and registered address of the company during the liquidation process.
A committee of inspection meeting was held to oversee the liquidation affairs of Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd.
A special resolution was passed to liquidate the subsidiary, NQF Limited.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director breaches fiduciary duties by orchestrating the transfer of significant asset sale proceeds to a specific investor group shortly before insolvency, and whether such transfers constitute voidable undue preferences.
Party_Details.Rule: A director must act in the interests of the company and its creditors as a whole when the company is in financial difficulty. Transactions that place one creditor in a better position than others within a defined statutory period prior to liquidation are voidable as undue preferences. Directors are prohibited from prioritizing the interests of an associate or specific investor over the collective pool of creditors.
Party_Details.Application: Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd and its subsidiary faced mounting debts and negative equity. Following the sale of the subsidiary's assets, a director—who also represented the recipient investor group—unilaterally removed other board members as bank signatories and transferred approximately NZ$2.7 million of the sale proceeds to his associated investor entities. These transfers occurred within six months of the company entering liquidation, while other substantial creditor claims remained unpaid and the company lacked funds to satisfy them.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The diverting of asset sale proceeds to a specific investor group was found to be a breach of fiduciary duties because the director acted out of loyalty to the investor rather than the company. The purported debt used to justify the transfer had already been satisfied through prior financial offsets, meaning there was no valid legal basis for the payment. Furthermore, because the transfers were made while the company was insolvent and occurred within the statutory six-month look-back period, they constituted an unfair preference that disadvantaged the general pool of creditors. The director was held liable to restore the funds to the liquidation estate.

Party_Details.Name: Townsing Henry George

Facts.Text: Henry George Townsing is an Australian individual.
At the material time, Henry George Townsing was a director of Newmans Group Holdings (Pty) Ltd, Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd and NQF Limited.
Henry George Townsing served as a director of Normandy Nominees Limited between 1995 and 1998.
Henry George Townsing acted as a representative of Normandy Nominees Limited.
Henry George Townsing served as a director of Normandy Finance & Investments Asia Ltd.
The principal activity of the Normandy Group is to invest in unlisted companies.
In late 2000, Henry George Townsing was informed of a possible investment opportunity in a New Zealand food manufacturing company for the Normandy Group.
Henry George Townsing was introduced to the chairmen and directors of Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd and NQF Limited to discuss potential investments.
Negotiations took place regarding an investment by the Normandy Group into Jenton Overseas Investment Pte Ltd.
On 13 February 2001, the director confirmed that the Normandy Group would subscribe for two million redeemable preference shares in Jenton.
The director joined Jenton's board as Normandy's nominee.
On 10 May 2001, the director advised bank lenders that the subsidiary, NQF, was not in a position to repay its current debts.
The managing director of NQF was removed, and consultants were retained to assist with a corporate turnaround.
The director informed associates that the terms of the investment and corporate structure would need to be renegotiated.
A new holding company, NGH, was incorporated in Australia to facilitate the restructuring of the investment.
A loan and option agreement was executed where Jenton's debt to Normandy was reassigned to the parent company, NGH.
A charge was executed by the parent company in favour of Normandy for the reassigned debt.
At the director's request, a charge was executed by the subsidiary, NQF, in favour of Normandy.
The director signed a subordination agreement on behalf of the subsidiary, prioritizing bank claims over Normandy's charge.
In April 2003, the subsidiary agreed to sell its operating assets and business.
In July 2003, the director presented a draft deed of rectification to amend the existing loan agreements, which other directors initially declined to sign.
Normandy demanded interest payments for the loans extended under the restructured agreements.
The company and the subsidiary disputed their liability to pay the demanded interest.
The subsidiary signed an option to sell its assets for NZ$3,737,500.
A final sale and purchase agreement for the subsidiary's assets was signed, with the price to be paid in two tranches.
A bank account was opened for the subsidiary with joint signatories to manage the expected sale proceeds.
Proposals were exchanged regarding the division of the sale proceeds, with suggestions to satisfy external creditors before paying the investor group.
Normandy issued a formal demand for $336,750 in interest from the parent, the company, and the subsidiary.
On Normandy's behalf, the director instructed legal counsel to review resolutions that would remove other directors and change the company's registered office.
The director proposed the appointment of a receiver and provisional liquidator to secure repayment to Normandy.
The director appointed a new company secretary.
The director appointed a new director to the company.
The director presented resolutions to the bank removing the other directors as signatories and making himself the sole signatory over the subsidiary's funds.
The director instructed the bank to transfer sums totaling NZ$2,707,300 from the subsidiary's Tauranga account to an Auckland account under his sole control.
The director facilitated the final transfer of NZ$1,692,054 to Normandy's UK account and NZ$985,246 to a related trust account.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a director breaches his statutory and fiduciary duties by prioritizing the repayment of an associated investor group over the company’s general creditors during a period of insolvency.
Party_Details.Rule: A director owes a statutory duty to act honestly and with reasonable diligence in the discharge of his duties. In the vicinity of insolvency, this fiduciary duty extends to the protection of the company's creditors. A director who uses his position to orchestrate a preferential payment to an associate while the company is unable to pay its debts is liable to restore those funds.
Party_Details.Application: A director of a distressed corporate group also served as a representative for the Normandy investment group. Shortly before the group entered liquidation, the director unilaterally removed other board members as bank signatories, assumed sole control over a major asset sale's proceeds, and transferred nearly NZ$2.7 million to his associated investor entities. This transfer occurred while the company faced significant unfulfilled obligations to other external creditors and was in a state of terminal financial distress.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: A director who prioritizes the recovery of an associate's investment over the company's collective creditor obligations fails to act honestly in the best interests of the company. The diverted funds were paid to satisfy a debt that had been legally extinguished through prior offsets, meaning there was no commercial justification for the payment. Because the director bypassed the board, manipulated account access, and executed the transfers while the company was insolvent, his actions constituted both a breach of fiduciary duty and an illegal undue preference. The director was found personally liable to compensate the company for the full amount of the misapplied proceeds.
